title: Install Pygame, Open a Window & Run the Game Loop
html:
Welcome to your path: games from scratch in Python

You finished Foundations and you chose this track on purpose. Good. This is the whole journey — no engine, no editor, just Python + Pygame, where you write every line that moves a pixel. Across these eight lessons you will build a real portfolio that spans genres: a bouncing-sprite toy, then Pong (arcade), Snake (grid), Space Invaders (shoot-em-up), a 2D platformer (gravity), a top-down dungeon shooter (Sprite Groups), a match-3 puzzle, and finally a polished, packaged capstone you can hand to anyone. Everything stands on its own. Let's get a window on the screen.

Install Python and Pygame

Pygame is a thin, friendly wrapper over SDL2. It hands you a window, a drawable frame buffer, input events, and audio. First make sure you have Python 3.9+ (python --version). Then install Pygame with pip. We use the maintained pygame-ce (Community Edition) — same import pygame, more fixes.

shell
# macOS / Linux python3 -m pip install pygame-ce # Windows py -m pip install pygame-ce # verify the install — opens a tiny demo python3 -m pygame.examples.aliens
Use a virtual environment.
Run python3 -m venv .venv then activate it (source .venv/bin/activate, or .venv\Scripts\activate on Windows). Installing into a venv keeps each game's dependencies clean and makes packaging in Lesson 8 painless.

The three pillars: init, display, loop

Every Pygame program does the same three things. It initializes the library, creates a display surface (the window you draw onto), and then spins a game loop that repeats forever: read input, update state, draw, repeat. Here is the smallest complete program that does all of it.

main.py
import pygame pygame.init() # start all subsystems screen = pygame.display.set_mode((640, 480))# the display surface pygame.display.set_caption('Hello Pygame') clock = pygame.time.Clock() # controls frame rate running = True while running: # 1) INPUT — drain the event queue for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False # 2) UPDATE — game logic goes here (nothing yet) # 3) DRAW — clear, then present screen.fill((20, 20, 30)) # dark blue-grey background pygame.display.flip() # swap buffers to screen clock.tick(60) # cap at 60 FPS pygame.quit()

What each line actually does

  • pygame.init() boots every subsystem (display, font, mixer, etc.). Call it once, first.
  • set_mode((w,h)) returns the display surface — a special Surface that maps to your window. Whatever you blit onto it appears after flip().
  • The event loop pygame.event.get() must run every frame. If you never drain the queue, the OS thinks your app froze and the window stops responding.
  • screen.fill(color) wipes last frame so you draw on a clean slate. Skip it and you get smeared trails.
  • display.flip() presents the finished frame. clock.tick(60) sleeps just enough to hold 60 frames per second.
Colors are RGB tuples.
Pygame uses (red, green, blue) with each channel 0–255. (255,0,0) is pure red, (0,0,0) black, (255,255,255) white. An optional 4th value is alpha (transparency).

delta time, briefly

clock.tick(60) returns the milliseconds the last frame took. Capture it as dt = clock.tick(60) and you can later make motion frame-rate independent by multiplying speeds by dt. For now the fixed 60 FPS cap is enough — we'll lean on dt when things start moving.

🎮 Build This: Toy — Bouncing Square

Genre: a toy (a tech demo, not yet a game). This starts the arc — it is brand new and everything later extends from these same three pillars.

  1. Open a 640×480 window titled Bouncer with the loop above.
  2. Make variables x, y = 100, 100 and vx, vy = 4, 3.
  3. In UPDATE, add velocity to position: x += vx, y += vy.
  4. If x < 0 or x > 600, flip horizontal direction: vx = -vx. Do the same for y with the height.
  5. In DRAW, after the fill, call pygame.draw.rect(screen, (255,200,0), (x, y, 40, 40)).
  6. Run it and watch a gold square bounce around the box. You just built your first frame-by-frame animation.

title: Drawing Shapes, Loading Images & Surfaces
html:
Everything is a Surface

In Pygame a Surface is just a rectangle of pixels you can draw onto. Your window is one Surface (the display surface). An image you load is another Surface. You build a frame by blitting (copying) smaller Surfaces onto the display surface, then calling flip(). Understand Surfaces and the rest of Pygame falls into place.

Drawing primitives

The pygame.draw module paints shapes directly onto any Surface. These are perfect for prototypes — Pong and Snake will be built almost entirely from rectangles.

draw.py
# signatures: pygame.draw.shape(surface, color, geometry, width=0) pygame.draw.rect(screen, (200,50,50), (30, 30, 120, 80)) # filled rect pygame.draw.rect(screen, (255,255,255), (30, 30, 120, 80), 3) # outline, 3px pygame.draw.circle(screen, (80,200,120), (320, 240), 40) # center, radius pygame.draw.line(screen, (255,220,0), (0,0), (640,480), 2) pygame.draw.polygon(screen, (120,120,255), [(400,100),(460,160),(400,220)])

A width of 0 (the default) fills the shape; any positive number draws only the outline at that thickness.

Loading images

Real games use art. pygame.image.load(path) returns a Surface from a PNG/JPG. Immediately call .convert() (or .convert_alpha() for transparency) — this re-encodes the image into the display's pixel format so every later blit is fast.

images.py
# load once, before the loop — never inside it! player_img = pygame.image.load('assets/ship.png').convert_alpha() bg_img = pygame.image.load('assets/sky.png').convert() # a Rect tracks the image's position and size player_rect = player_img.get_rect(center=(320, 240)) # ... inside the DRAW phase of the loop ... screen.blit(bg_img, (0, 0)) # draw background first screen.blit(player_img, player_rect) # then the ship on top
Load outside the loop.
image.load hits the disk. Doing it every frame will tank your FPS and may crash. Load all art and sound once at startup, store the Surfaces in variables, and reuse them.

Transforming Surfaces

The pygame.transform module returns new Surfaces — scaled, rotated, or flipped. These are also relatively expensive, so cache results when you can rather than recomputing each frame.

transform.py
big = pygame.transform.scale(player_img, (96, 96)) spun = pygame.transform.rotate(player_img, 45) # degrees, CCW mirror = pygame.transform.flip(player_img, True, False) # flip x, not y

Text with Fonts

You'll need a score readout in almost every game. Create a Font once, then render the text to a Surface and blit it.

text.py
font = pygame.font.SysFont('consolas', 32) # or Font(None, 32) label = font.render('Score: 0', True, (255,255,255)) # antialias=True screen.blit(label, (16, 16))
Draw order = paint order.
Pygame has no z-index. Whatever you blit last sits on top. Background first, gameplay objects next, UI/text last.
🎮 Build This: Toy — Bouncing Sprite (upgraded)

Genre: still a toy. This extends Lesson 1's Bouncing Square — swap the bare rectangle for a real image and add a HUD, so you practice loading, blitting, and text.

  1. Find or make a small PNG (a ball or ship). Load it with .convert_alpha() before the loop.
  2. Replace the draw.rect from Lesson 1 with screen.blit(img, (x, y)).
  3. Use img.get_rect() to read the image's real width/height for the wall-bounce checks instead of hard-coded 40s.
  4. Add a SysFont and render a live counter of how many times it has bounced; blit it top-left.
  5. Draw a 3px white outline rectangle as a 'frame' around the play area so the bouncing reads clearly.

title: Events, Keyboard & Mouse Input
html:
Two ways to read input

Pygame gives you input in two flavors, and good games use both. Events are discrete one-shot facts — 'the A key went down this frame', 'the window closed', 'a mouse button was clicked'. State polling asks 'is the A key held down right now?' every frame. Use events for actions that fire once (jump, shoot, pause) and polling for continuous things (walking, holding a paddle up).

The event queue

Each frame, pygame.event.get() hands you every event since last frame. You must loop over all of them. Match on event.type; key events carry event.key, mouse events carry event.pos and event.button.

events.py
for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False elif event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN: if event.key == pygame.K_SPACE: shoot() # fires exactly once per press elif event.key == pygame.K_ESCAPE: running = False elif event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN: if event.button == 1: # 1=left, 2=middle, 3=right place_block(event.pos) # event.pos is (x, y)

Polling held keys

For movement you want to know what is held right now. pygame.key.get_pressed() returns a big boolean sequence indexed by key constant. Read it once per frame in your UPDATE phase.

polling.py
keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() if keys[pygame.K_LEFT] or keys[pygame.K_a]: player_x -= speed if keys[pygame.K_RIGHT] or keys[pygame.K_d]: player_x += speed # diagonal works for free — both branches can run the same frame
Events fire once, polling repeats.
Put jump/shoot/menu-select in KEYDOWN so one tap = one action. Put run/steer in get_pressed() so holding keeps it going. Mixing these up is the #1 beginner input bug.

The mouse

pygame.mouse.get_pos() gives the current cursor (x, y); pygame.mouse.get_pressed() returns (left, middle, right) booleans. Combine cursor position with a click event to build buttons and aim weapons.

mouse.py
mx, my = pygame.mouse.get_pos() button_rect = pygame.Rect(270, 200, 100, 40) hovering = button_rect.collidepoint(mx, my) color = (90,160,255) if hovering else (60,60,90) pygame.draw.rect(screen, color, button_rect)

Common key constants

ConstantKey
K_UP K_DOWN K_LEFT K_RIGHTArrow keys
K_w K_a K_s K_dWASD movement
K_SPACE K_RETURNSpace / Enter
K_ESCAPEEscape (quit/pause)
🎮 Build This: Arcade — PONG

Genre: arcade (NEW GENRE). This starts Pong from a fresh file, but reuses your loop, drawing, and the ball-bounce logic from the toy. It is your first true game with a player and a win condition.

  1. Make two paddle rects (left and right, each ~10×80) and a ball rect (~14×14) centered.
  2. Left paddle: poll K_w/K_s. Right paddle: poll K_UP/K_DOWN. Clamp paddles inside the screen.
  3. Move the ball each frame with ball_vx, ball_vy; bounce off top/bottom walls by negating ball_vy.
  4. When the ball's rect overlaps a paddle rect, negate ball_vx (you'll formalize this next lesson — for now paddle.colliderect(ball) works).
  5. If the ball exits left or right, award a point to the other side and recenter the ball. Render both scores with a Font.

title: Rects, Vectors & Grid-Based Movement
html:
The Rect: Pygame's workhorse

A pygame.Rect stores an axis-aligned rectangle as (x, y, width, height) and gives you dozens of helpers for positioning, alignment, and overlap tests. Almost everything you move on screen will be a Rect or own one. Master it and movement, collision, and layout all get easy.

rects.py
r = pygame.Rect(100, 50, 40, 40) # x, y, w, h r.x += 5 # move right r.center = (320, 240) # snap center to a point r.right = 640 # align right edge to the wall r.bottom = 480 # readable edges & anchors: # r.left r.top r.right r.bottom r.centerx r.centery r.topleft ... moved = r.move(10, 0) # returns a NEW shifted Rect inside = r.clamp(screen.get_rect()) # keep r inside another rect
Anchors keep layout clean.
Instead of math to center something, set the anchor: label_rect = label.get_rect(center=screen.get_rect().center). Setting any anchor moves the whole Rect; the size stays fixed.

Smooth movement with Vector2

Rects store integers, so they can't hold fractional speed like 2.5 px/frame. For smooth motion, keep a float Vector2 position and copy it into the Rect each frame. pygame.math.Vector2 also gives you length, normalize, and rotation — essential for the platformer and the dungeon shooter later.

vectors.py
from pygame.math import Vector2 pos = Vector2(100, 100) vel = Vector2(2.5, -1.5) pos += vel # float-accurate motion rect.center = (round(pos.x), round(pos.y)) # sync the Rect # aim a bullet toward the mouse at constant speed: direction = (Vector2(mx, my) - pos) if direction.length() > 0: bullet_vel = direction.normalize() * 8

Grid-based movement

Many genres think in tiles, not pixels: Snake, Sokoban, match-3, tile maps. Pick a TILE size, store positions in grid coordinates (col, row), and multiply by TILE only when drawing. Movement becomes adding ±1 to a coordinate.

grid.py
TILE = 24 head = (5, 5) # grid coords (col, row) d = (1, 0) # moving right one tile head = (head[0] + d[0], head[1] + d[1]) # draw a grid cell as a pixel rect: px, py = head[0] * TILE, head[1] * TILE pygame.draw.rect(screen, (80,220,120), (px, py, TILE, TILE))
Step the grid on a timer, not every frame.
60 grid-steps/second is unplayable. Use a counter: increment each frame and only advance the snake when it crosses a threshold (e.g., every 8 frames), or use pygame.time.get_ticks() to step every 120 ms.

Timed steps

timer.py
move_every = 120 # milliseconds per grid step last_move = 0 # ... in UPDATE ... now = pygame.time.get_ticks() if now - last_move >= move_every: last_move = now advance_snake()
🎮 Build This: Grid — SNAKE

Genre: grid arcade (NEW GENRE). This starts Snake in a new file. It cements Rects, grid coordinates, and timed stepping — none of which Pong needed.

  1. Define TILE=24 and a board (e.g., 25×20 tiles). Store the snake as a list of (col,row) tuples, head first.
  2. Read KEYDOWN arrows to set a direction vector; reject reversing directly into yourself.
  3. On each timed step, prepend a new head = old head + direction, and pop the tail (unless food was eaten).
  4. Spawn food at a random empty tile. When the head lands on it, grow (skip the pop) and increment score.
  5. Game over if the head hits a wall or any body segment. Draw each segment and the food as TILE-sized rects, and show the score with a Font.

title: Collision Detection: Rects, Lists & Masks
html:
Why collision is the heart of gameplay

Bullets hitting aliens, a snake biting itself, a player landing on a platform — all collision. Pygame gives you a ladder of techniques from cheap-and-approximate to pixel-perfect. Pick the cheapest one that feels right; reach for masks only when rectangles lie to the player.

Rect vs Rect

The everyday test is rect.colliderect(other) — true if two rectangles overlap. rect.collidepoint(x, y) tests a single point (great for mouse-on-button and click-to-place).

rectcoll.py
if player_rect.colliderect(enemy_rect): take_damage() if button_rect.collidepoint(pygame.mouse.get_pos()): highlight()

One thing vs many things

Shoot-em-ups test one bullet against a whole list of enemies. rect.collidelist(rects) returns the index of the first hit (or -1); collidelistall returns every hit index. For lists of objects, just iterate.

manycoll.py
# iterate safely while removing — loop over a copy for bullet in bullets[:]: bullet.y -= 10 for enemy in enemies[:]: if bullet.colliderect(enemy): bullets.remove(bullet) enemies.remove(enemy) score += 10 break # this bullet is spent
Never mutate a list you're iterating.
Removing from a list mid-loop skips elements or crashes. Loop over a shallow copy (for x in items[:]) and remove from the original, or collect doomed items and remove them after the loop.

Resolving collisions, not just detecting

Detection says that they touched; resolution decides what to do. For platforms, the classic trick is to move on one axis at a time and push the player back out using the platform's edge — this is exactly what the platformer needs.

resolve.py
# move horizontally, then fix overlaps player.x += vel_x for wall in walls: if player.colliderect(wall): if vel_x > 0: player.right = wall.left # hit left side elif vel_x < 0: player.left = wall.right # hit right side # then repeat the same pattern for the vertical axis

Pixel-perfect masks

Rectangles over-approximate round ships and diagonal sprites — players notice 'phantom' hits in the transparent corners. A pygame.mask.Mask records which pixels are solid (non-transparent). Build one with from_surface, then test the offset between two masks.

masks.py
mask_a = pygame.mask.from_surface(ship_img) mask_b = pygame.mask.from_surface(rock_img) offset = (rock_rect.x - ship_rect.x, rock_rect.y - ship_rect.y) if mask_a.overlap(mask_b, offset): explode() # only true when solid pixels actually touch
Cheap test first, then refine.
Masks are slower. Do a fast colliderect as a broad-phase gate; only run the mask overlap when the rectangles already intersect. You get pixel accuracy at almost no average cost.
🎮 Build This: Shoot-em-up — SPACE INVADERS

Genre: shoot-em-up (NEW GENRE). This starts Space Invaders in a new file, putting list-collision and the bullet/enemy loop to work. (Optional: add masks for satisfying pixel-perfect hits.)

  1. Build a grid of ~5×8 enemy rects that slide as a block left/right, dropping down and reversing at the edges.
  2. The player ship at the bottom moves with arrows/AD; KEYDOWN Space appends a bullet rect at the ship's top.
  3. Each frame move bullets up; use the safe copy-loop to remove bullets that exit or hit an enemy, and remove hit enemies, adding score.
  4. Let enemies occasionally drop bombs; if a bomb's rect collides the player, lose a life.
  5. Win when all enemies are cleared; lose when an enemy reaches the bottom or lives hit zero. Show score and lives with a Font.

title: The Sprite Class, Groups & Gravity
html:
Why Sprite and Group exist

Tracking parallel lists of rects, images, and velocities gets messy fast. Pygame's pygame.sprite.Sprite bundles an image and a rect into one object with its own update() behavior, and pygame.sprite.Group manages a collection of them — updating, drawing, and collision-checking the whole set with one call. This is how you keep big games like a dungeon shooter sane.

A Sprite subclass

Subclass Sprite, set self.image and self.rect in __init__, and put per-frame logic in update(). The Group will call update() on every member for you.

player.py
class Player(pygame.sprite.Sprite): def __init__(self, x, y): super().__init__() self.image = pygame.image.load('hero.png').convert_alpha() self.rect = self.image.get_rect(midbottom=(x, y)) self.vel = pygame.math.Vector2(0, 0) self.on_ground = False def update(self, walls): keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() self.vel.x = (keys[pygame.K_d] - keys[pygame.K_a]) * 5 self.vel.y += 0.6 # GRAVITY pulls down if keys[pygame.K_SPACE] and self.on_ground: self.vel.y = -13 # JUMP impulse self._move(walls) def _move(self, walls): self.rect.x += round(self.vel.x) for w in walls: if self.rect.colliderect(w): if self.vel.x > 0: self.rect.right = w.left elif self.vel.x < 0: self.rect.left = w.right self.on_ground = False self.rect.y += round(self.vel.y) for w in walls: if self.rect.colliderect(w): if self.vel.y > 0: self.rect.bottom = w.top; self.on_ground = True elif self.vel.y < 0: self.rect.top = w.bottom self.vel.y = 0
Gravity in three lines.
Add a constant to vel.y every frame (acceleration), apply velocity to position, and zero vel.y when you land. That's a complete platformer physics core — no math library needed.

Groups do the busywork

Add sprites to a Group and it updates and draws them all at once. Group.draw(surface) blits each sprite's image at its rect; Group.update(*args) forwards arguments to every update().

groups.py
all_sprites = pygame.sprite.Group() bullets = pygame.sprite.Group() enemies = pygame.sprite.Group() player = Player(100, 300); all_sprites.add(player) # in UPDATE: all_sprites.update() # group-vs-group collision, auto-removing the killed sprites: hits = pygame.sprite.groupcollide(bullets, enemies, True, True) score += 10 * len(hits) # one sprite vs a group: if pygame.sprite.spritecollideany(player, enemies): player_hit() # in DRAW: all_sprites.draw(screen)
HelperUse
spritecollide(s, group, dokill)list of sprites in group hitting s
groupcollide(g1, g2, k1, k2)every cross-hit; optionally kill both sides
spritecollideany(s, group)fast 'did s hit anything?' check
sprite.kill()remove a sprite from all its groups
Pass collided=... for masks.
The collide helpers accept collided=pygame.sprite.collide_mask to do pixel-perfect group collisions, combining Lesson 5's masks with Groups in one line.
🎮 Build This: Platformer — Tiny Jump Quest

Genre: 2D platformer (NEW GENRE). This starts a platformer in a new file using the gravity Player above, then you'll also reuse Groups next for the dungeon shooter.

  1. Use the Player class with gravity and jump. Build a level as a list of wall rects (ground + a few floating platforms).
  2. Stay on one screen first: place coins (small sprites) the player collects via spritecollide(player, coins, True).
  3. Add spike rects; touching one respawns the player at the start and costs a life.
  4. Add a goal flag rect; reaching it shows 'You Win!'. Track coins collected with a Font HUD.
  5. Dungeon teaser: spin up a second mini-scene with a top-down Player (remove gravity, WASD 8-way), an enemies Group, and a bullets Group fired toward the mouse using Lesson 4's normalize() — proving Groups carry you across genres.

title: Sound, Music & a Scene/State System
html:
Audio: the mixer

Sound is the cheapest way to make a game feel alive. Pygame's mixer handles short sound effects (Sound objects, loaded fully into memory) and long streaming music (music, streamed from disk so one big file doesn't eat RAM). Initialize the mixer before loading audio — pygame.init() already does, but calling pre_init first lowers latency.

audio.py
pygame.mixer.pre_init(44100, -16, 2, 512) # low-latency buffer pygame.init() shoot_sfx = pygame.mixer.Sound('assets/shoot.wav') shoot_sfx.set_volume(0.4) shoot_sfx.play() # fire-and-forget pygame.mixer.music.load('assets/theme.ogg') pygame.mixer.music.set_volume(0.5) pygame.mixer.music.play(-1) # -1 = loop forever
WAV for SFX, OGG for music.
Short effects load best as small .wav files; long tracks belong in compressed .ogg streamed via music. MP3 support varies by platform — OGG is the safe, free choice.

Why you need scenes

So far each game has been one big loop. Real games have a title menu, the gameplay, a pause overlay, and a game-over screen. Cramming all of that into one loop with tangled if flags becomes unmaintainable. The fix is a scene (state) system: each screen is its own object that knows how to handle input, update, and draw itself.

A clean Scene base class

scene.py
class Scene: next_scene = None # set to request a switch def handle(self, event): pass def update(self): pass def draw(self, screen): pass class MenuScene(Scene): def handle(self, event): if event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN and event.key == pygame.K_RETURN: self.next_scene = GameScene() # start playing def draw(self, screen): screen.fill((15,15,30)) draw_centered(screen, 'PRESS ENTER') class GameScene(Scene): def __init__(self): self.score = 0; self.sprites = pygame.sprite.Group() def update(self): self.sprites.update() if self.player_dead: self.next_scene = GameOverScene(self.score) def draw(self, screen): screen.fill((10,10,15)) self.sprites.draw(screen)

The driver loop owns the current scene

Your main() holds one reference to the active scene and just forwards events, update, and draw to it. When a scene sets next_scene, you swap. Adding a new screen never touches existing code.

driver.py
scene = MenuScene() while running: for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False scene.handle(event) scene.update() scene.draw(screen) pygame.display.flip() clock.tick(60) if scene.next_scene: # a switch was requested scene = scene.next_scene
Pause is just a scene that remembers the previous one.
Store self.paused_under = current_scene when entering a PauseScene; on resume, set next_scene back to it. Because the gameplay scene isn't updated while paused, it simply freezes in place.
🎮 Build This: Puzzle — MATCH-3 (Gem Crush)

Genre: match-3 puzzle (NEW GENRE). This starts a puzzle game in a new file and is the first one built with the scene system and audio from day one.

  1. Make a 2D list grid of colored gems (reuse Lesson 4's tile drawing). Wrap it in a GameScene.
  2. Click to select a gem, click an adjacent one to swap (mouse events from Lesson 3).
  3. After a swap, scan rows and columns for runs of 3+ same-color gems; clear them, play a Sound, and add score.
  4. Collapse gems down into empty cells and refill the top with new random gems; re-scan for chain reactions.
  5. Wrap everything in a MenuScene -> GameScene -> GameOverScene flow (game over on a move limit), with looping music playing throughout.

title: Capstone: Polish, Menu, Score & Packaging with PyInstaller
html:
From prototype to portfolio piece

You've built across six genres. The final step turns one of them into something you can put your name on and hand to anyone — no Python required. That means three things: a complete game-feel loop (menu -> play -> game over -> restart), real polish (juice, persistent high score), and a single distributable executable built with PyInstaller.

Pick one and finish it

Choose the game you love most — Space Invaders, the platformer, the dungeon shooter, or match-3. Fold in the scene system from Lesson 7 so it has a title screen and a game-over screen with a restart prompt. A polished loop is what separates a demo from a game.

Polish: juice and persistence

Small touches read as 'professional'. Add screen shake on hits, a particle burst on explosions, a tween on score numbers, and — crucially — a high score saved to disk so progress persists between runs.

polish.py
import json, random, os # --- persistent high score --- SAVE = 'highscore.json' def load_high(): if os.path.exists(SAVE): return json.load(open(SAVE)).get('high', 0) return 0 def save_high(v): json.dump({'high': v}, open(SAVE, 'w')) # --- screen shake: offset the whole frame for a few frames --- shake = 0 def on_explosion(): global shake; shake = 12 # in DRAW, render the world to a buffer then blit it shifted: off = (random.randint(-shake, shake), random.randint(-shake, shake)) if shake else (0,0) screen.blit(world_surf, off) if shake > 0: shake -= 1
Restart without leaks.
Make 'restart' build a fresh GameScene() rather than mutating the old one. New Groups, new score, new player — no stale state carried over. The scene system makes this one line.

Load assets so they survive packaging

When PyInstaller bundles your app, the working directory changes. Resolve asset paths relative to the script (and PyInstaller's temp dir) so loads don't fail in the .exe.

resource_path.py
import sys, os def resource_path(rel): base = getattr(sys, '_MEIPASS', os.path.abspath('.')) return os.path.join(base, rel) # use it everywhere you load: img = pygame.image.load(resource_path('assets/ship.png')).convert_alpha()

Package with PyInstaller

pyinstaller freezes your script and its dependencies into a standalone build. --onefile produces a single executable; --windowed (a.k.a. --noconsole) hides the terminal; --add-data bundles your assets folder.

shell
python3 -m pip install pyinstaller # Windows (note the ; path separator) pyinstaller --onefile --windowed --add-data 'assets;assets' main.py # macOS / Linux (note the : path separator) pyinstaller --onefile --windowed --add-data 'assets:assets' main.py # result lands in dist/ — ship that file dist/main # or dist/main.exe on Windows
Build on the OS you target.
PyInstaller does not cross-compile. A Windows .exe must be built on Windows; a macOS .app on macOS. Use a clean venv when building so only the libraries you actually use get bundled, keeping the binary small.
Ship the source too.
For a portfolio, a recruiter wants to read your code. Put the project on GitHub with a short README (controls, a GIF, how to run), the requirements.txt (pip freeze > requirements.txt), and a download link to the packaged build. The polish lives in the repo as much as the binary.
🎮 Build This: CAPSTONE — Finish & Ship One Game

Genre: your choice — the portfolio capstone. This extends one earlier game (Invaders, platformer, dungeon shooter, or match-3) into a complete, polished, packaged product. This is the deliverable that proves the whole track.

  1. Pick your favorite earlier game and wrap it in the Lesson 7 scene system: Menu -> Game -> Game Over, with restart and quit.
  2. Add sound effects and looping music, a live score HUD, and a persistent high score saved to JSON.
  3. Add at least two pieces of juice (screen shake, particles, a hit flash, or score pop).
  4. Route every asset load through resource_path() so the bundle works.
  5. Build a standalone executable with PyInstaller, test it on a clean machine/folder, and publish the repo with a README, a GIF, and the download. You now have a packaged, portfolio-ready game built entirely from scratch.
